moment_text: Yeah, it was actually a really strange experience. I remember one of my coworkers was talking about how she was BRCA positive, went to the surgeon and like ran away and was like, there's no way I am doing this, um, uh, preemptively. And then, uh, my boss, one of my bosses got diagnosed with, um, breast cancer and then my friend was visiting me from. College. And you know, like when we're fun, like when we get together we just, it's like one of those friends where we just do stupid things and we were actually like comparing our breasts in the mirror, like teenagers. And now looking back it's like such a bittersweet, tragic story because she was like, she was like, oh my gosh, Ana, you gotta get out there. And we went to this wedding together of like one of my friends was getting married and then after, I remember, after I came back, she left. And I don't know, something about that equation, just like I had never done a self-breast exam and then I just decided to, to do it. And then I felt two small, one was like so small lumps, which would later turn out to be three tumors and. As soon as I felt that, like I knew something was wrong and I was just like, okay. They have kind of been feeling weird. But I don't know, I was really, I had very, very large, dense breasts and I always have. So it was hard to know what was going on. 'cause they always felt rather heavy and, you know, swollen during my period, whatnot. Um, I would say the thing I struggle with the most is like asking for logistical help. 'cause that's what I need the most and I don't know how to get it, so I don't have an answer for how to get it. But I think asking for it, like driving's like really hard on my body now and I have a lot of like hip tension and pelvic tension from the treatment. I wish like more people could drive me, but if they want, like that's a very simple way in which they could help.
eligibility_rationale: This moment contains clear barrier signals (lack_of_support) and agency signals (seeking_support) related to the challenges of post-treatment life, making it eligible for pattern analysis.

moment_text: And then I guess, I dunno if this is helpful, but my brother of all people told me this because I was really, your body is just changing constantly. Like, and I really struggled with, after I got my expanders out and had to get my second exchange surgery, I was like grieving almost, because, so my breasts are a lot smaller than they were before. And um, he was like, well, what if you just look at it like a relationship, like you're on this temporary relationship with, with this and I want, all I want is stability, more stability and a new normal. And everyone's like, you're gonna get to this other side. You're gonna feel the new normal. Like, I have not, I, I have not. And so like that logic is not helpful for me anymore. I just try to think of it as more of that like really just one day at a time. I can think of the next week I can think of, have loose goals for my year and that's it. Like, just don't put so much pressure on yourself for finding, you know, I, I think also as a young person, everyone's obsessed with like identity politics and like, oh, but I'm like this and I'm not like this and I'm not, that's gonna change with the hormones with this, with that, I just focus on that stuff less and I just kind of try to go about my world. It might make you like a little bit of a weirdo, but you know what? You can, you can find inspiration and help to in all sorts of sources. For me it was movies and music when I felt like people weren't giving me that. So look outside of that, that saved me during the pandemic when I literally could not touch or like interact with humans is like, that's who were my friends, like the X-Men and, and like weird and I watched like Monster and the Sound of Metal and all these crazy movies. I don't know, I would just say. Just constantly try to find, just keep, keep looking for people who and things, and that make you feel seen and heard. And if you look hard enough, you'll probably find it. It's a lot of work, but it's out there.
